items.instruction: Which event is one of the listed times to turn the REIL off?
items.question_text: Under 3-4-2, after operating REIL with separate on-off controls, which event is one of the listed triggers for turning the lights off?

choices.text: The runway edge lights are set to step 2.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-2a1, one listed trigger for turning REIL off is that an arriving aircraft has landed.

items.instruction: Use Table 3-4-1 to answer this question.
items.question_text: Under Table 3-4-1, which REIL intensity setting applies during the day when visibility is less than 2 miles?
items.explanation: Table 3-4-1 shows REIL setting 3 for day visibility less than 2 miles.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A pilot requested the REIL, the associated runway lights are lighted, and a departure has just taken off but remains in the traffic pattern area. Another controller wants the REIL turned off immediately.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-2, when separate on-off controls are provided, REIL are operated with the associated runway lights and may be turned off after a departure has left the traffic pattern area or when the lights are of no further use to the pilot.

choices.text: Keep the REIL on until the departure leaves the traffic pattern area or the lights are no longer needed.
Turn the REIL off as soon as the aircraft lifts off.
Turn the REIL off because pilot requests never affect REIL operation.
Leave the REIL off unless the runway edge lights are extinguished.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-2a1, one listed trigger for turning REIL off after departure is that the departing aircraft has left the traffic pattern area.

items.instruction: Does this SFL statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers must operate sequenced flashing lights when visibility is less than 3 miles and instrument approaches are being made to the runway served by the associated ALS.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-4-7a.

items.instruction: Which circumstance is one of the listed SFL operating bases?
items.question_text: Under 3-4-7, which circumstance is explicitly listed as a basis for operating sequenced flashing lights?

choices.text: Only when the ALS is off.
Only after a PAR controller approves the change.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-7b, SFL may be operated as requested by the pilot.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Instrument approaches are being made to the runway served by the ALS, the visibility is 2 miles, and the pilot has not asked for the sequenced flashing lights to be extinguished. Another controller says to leave the SFL off because the ALS is already on.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-7, sequenced flashing lights are operated when visibility is less than 3 miles and instrument approaches are being made, as requested by the pilot, or as you deem necessary if not contrary to the pilot's request.

choices.text: Leave the SFL off because the ALS being on is enough.
Operate the SFL only if visibility falls below 1 mile.
Delay using SFL until the aircraft passes the threshold.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-7a, the listed visibility and approach conditions call for operating the SFL.

items.instruction: Use Table 3-4-7 to answer this question.
items.question_text: Under the three-step MALS/RAIL/ODALS table, which setting applies during the day when visibility is 2 to 5 miles inclusive?
items.explanation: Table 3-4-7 shows setting 2 for day visibility 2 to 5 miles inclusive.

items.instruction: Does this MALSR/ODALS statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: For a three-step MALS/RAIL/ODALS system at a part-time tower, if there is no radio air-to-ground control when the tower is not staffed during darkness, the MALSR/ODALS must be set on intensity setting 2.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in the note under Table 3-4-7.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A part-time tower closes for the night. The airport uses the three-step MALSR/ODALS table, duplicate controls are not provided in the FSS, and there is no radio air-to-ground control available after the tower closes.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-8, the three-step MALSR/ODALS note requires intensity setting 2 during darkness when the tower is not staffed if there is no radio air-to-ground control.

choices.text: Set the MALSR/ODALS to intensity setting 2 during darkness.
Set the MALSR/ODALS to the highest setting all night.
Turn the MALSR/ODALS off because the tower is closed.
Use low intensity only if a pilot calls from the air.

items.explanation: The note under Table 3-4-7 requires setting 2 in that situation.

items.instruction: Does this ALSF-2/SSALR statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers must operate the ALSF-2 system as requested by the pilot when prevailing visibility is 3/4 mile or less, or the RVR is 4,000 feet or less.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-4-9a1.

items.instruction: Which lighting action matches this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 3-4-9, what action applies when the conditions in subparagraph a are not a factor?

choices.text: Operate the ALSF-2 system at maximum intensity.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-9b, SSALR is operated when the conditions in subparagraph a are not a factor.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Visibility improves above 3/4 mile and RVR rises above 4,000 feet, so the low-visibility conditions in subparagraph a are no longer a factor. Another controller wants to keep operating the ALSF-2 system under the same low-visibility logic.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-9, ALSF-2 is operated under the low-visibility conditions in subparagraph a, and SSALR is operated when those conditions are not a factor.

choices.text: Keep using ALSF-2 solely because it was already on.
Turn the approach lighting system off completely.
Wait for the pilot to request a lighting change before doing anything.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-9b, SSALR applies when the subparagraph a conditions are not a factor.

items.instruction: Use Table 3-4-8 to answer this question.
items.question_text: Under Table 3-4-8, which HIRL/RCLS/TDZL step applies at night when visibility is 3 to 5 miles inclusive?

choices.text: When requested only, with no assigned step.

items.explanation: Table 3-4-8 shows step 2 for night visibility 3 to 5 miles inclusive.

items.instruction: Which item is one of the listed exceptions to Table 3-4-8?
items.question_text: Under 3-4-11, which item is one of the stated exceptions to operating in accordance with Table 3-4-8?

choices.text: Only when the approach controller approves the change.
Whenever the aircraft is within 3 miles of touchdown.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-11b, one listed exception is operation as requested by the pilot.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Night visibility is 4 miles, but the pilot requests a brighter HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting than the one shown in Table 3-4-8. Another controller says the table can never be varied.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-11, high intensity runway, runway centerline, and touchdown zone lights are operated in accordance with Table 3-4-8, except where a facility directive specifies other settings, as requested by the pilot, or as you deem necessary if not contrary to pilot request.

choices.text: Honor the pilot-requested setting because pilot request is a listed exception.
Refuse any change because Table 3-4-8 has no exceptions.
Dim the lights to step 1 regardless of the request.
Turn the associated lights off until visibility drops below 1 mile.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-11b, pilot request is one of the listed exceptions to the table.

items.instruction: Use Table 3-4-9 to answer this question.
items.question_text: Under Table 3-4-9, which HIRL-associated-with-MALSR step applies at night when visibility is more than 5 miles?
items.explanation: Table 3-4-9 shows step 1 for night visibility more than 5 miles.

items.instruction: Does this brightness-control statement match the note?
items.question_text: When moving from a given HIRL/MALSR brightness setting to a lower setting, controllers must rotate the control below the intended setting and then back to the appropriate step to ensure proper MALSR brightness.
items.explanation: That note appears in 3-4-12.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You need to lower the HIRL setting that also controls the associated MALSR from a brighter step to a lower step. A trainee proposes rotating the control directly to the target step and stopping there.
items.para_context: The note in 3-4-12 explains that when going from a given brightness step to a lower setting, rotating below the intended step and then back to the correct step ensures the MALSR will operate at the appropriate brightness.

choices.text: Rotate below the intended step, then bring the control back to the target step.
Rotate directly to the target step and stop there.
Turn the MALSR off and back on instead of selecting a lower step.
Leave the lights at the brighter step because lower settings are not authorized.

items.explanation: The note in 3-4-12 gives the required control movement to ensure proper MALSR brightness.

items.instruction: Is this HIRL/RVR directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Keep the appropriate approach controller or PAR controller informed, in advance if possible, of HIRL changes that affect RVR.
items.explanation: That statement is the full requirement in 3-4-13.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A change to high intensity runway lights will affect RVR. The approach controller has not yet been told, but there is still enough time to coordinate before the change takes effect.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-13, keep the appropriate approach controller or PAR controller informed, in advance if possible, of HIRL changes that affect RVR.

choices.text: Inform the appropriate approach controller or PAR controller in advance if possible.
Wait until after the change takes effect so the coordination reflects the final condition.
Notify only the center controller because RVR coordination is an en route function.
Skip the coordination unless the pilot asks about the lighting change.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires informing the appropriate approach controller or PAR controller, in advance if possible.

items.instruction: Is this glidepath-notification directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Inform the aircraft when it is approaching glidepath, approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 5-12-1.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An aircraft is approximately 10 to 30 seconds from final descent on the PAR. A trainee asks whether to wait until descent actually starts before issuing the glidepath advisory.
items.para_context: Under 5-12-1, the aircraft is informed when it is approaching glidepath, approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft that it is approaching glidepath.
Wait until final descent begins, then issue the first glidepath advisory.
Delay the advisory until the aircraft reaches decision height.
Skip the advisory unless the pilot specifically requests it.

items.explanation: Per 5-12-1, the glidepath notification is given before final descent, not after it begins.

items.instruction: Is this descent instruction stated correctly?
items.question_text: When an aircraft reaches the point where final descent is to start, instruct it to begin descent.
items.explanation: That is the full requirement in 5-12-3.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An aircraft has just reached the point where final descent is to start on the PAR. Another controller suggests waiting a little longer before telling the aircraft to descend so the call will be closer to touchdown.
items.para_context: Under 5-12-3, when the aircraft reaches the point where final descent is to start, it is instructed to begin descent.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to begin descent now.
Delay the descent instruction until the aircraft is over the threshold.
Wait until decision height before issuing the descent instruction.
Omit the descent instruction because PAR aircraft descend on their own.

items.explanation: Per 5-12-3, the instruction is issued when the aircraft reaches the point where final descent is to start.

items.instruction: Which advisory is explicitly listed in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 5-12-7, which advisory is specifically given when the aircraft is passing over the approach lights?
items.explanation: That phraseology appears in 5-12-7b.

items.instruction: Does this position-advisory statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers must continue to provide glidepath and course information prescribed in paragraph 5-12-4, subparagraphs a and b, until the aircraft passes over threshold.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-12-7a.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A PAR aircraft is passing over the landing threshold and is slightly right of the final approach course. A trainee thinks the advisories stop once the aircraft reaches the threshold area.
items.para_context: Under 5-12-7, the aircraft is informed when it is passing over the landing threshold and is advised of its position with respect to the final approach course.

choices.text: Advise that the aircraft is over the landing threshold and state its position with respect to course.
Stop all advisories because threshold crossing ends the paragraph's required calls.
Issue communications transfer instructions instead of a position advisory.
Repeat the approach-lights advisory because threshold advisories are not authorized.

items.explanation: Per 5-12-7c, the aircraft is advised over the landing threshold and told its position with respect to course.

items.instruction: Is this communication-transfer directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Issue communications transfer instructions.
items.explanation: That is the body requirement in 5-12-9.

items.instruction: Does this note match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Communications transfer instructions should be delayed slightly until the aircraft is on the landing roll-out to preclude diversion of the pilot's attention during transition and touchdown.
items.explanation: That note appears in 5-12-9.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A PAR aircraft is transitioning and touching down. You still need to issue the communications transfer instruction, but doing so immediately would pull the pilot's attention away during touchdown.
items.para_context: Under 5-12-9, communications transfer instructions are issued, but the note says they should be delayed slightly until the aircraft is on the landing roll-out to avoid diverting the pilot's attention during transition and touchdown.

choices.text: Delay the transfer instruction slightly until the aircraft is on the landing roll-out.
Issue the transfer instruction during transition even if it distracts the pilot.
Wait until the aircraft is parked before issuing any transfer instruction.
Omit the transfer instruction entirely because PAR aircraft never receive one.

items.explanation: The note in 5-12-9 directs a slight delay until landing roll-out to avoid pilot distraction.

items.instruction: Does this surveillance-unusable statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: PAR approaches may be conducted when the ASR is unusable if a nonradar instrument approach will position the aircraft over a NAVAID or DME fix within precision radar coverage, or if an adjacent radar facility can provide a direct radar handoff to the PAR controller.
items.explanation: That statement appears in the body of 5-12-11.

items.instruction: Does this note correctly state the referenced identification rule?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-3-2, Primary Radar Identification Methods.
items.explanation: The note in 5-12-11 cites paragraph 5-3-2 and explains that display of the NAVAID or DME fix under that paragraph is not required if the fix can be correlated on a PAR scope.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: The ASR is unusable, but an adjacent radar facility can provide a direct radar handoff to the PAR controller. A trainee believes PAR approaches must be suspended anytime the ASR is unavailable.
items.para_context: Under 5-12-11, PAR approaches may still be conducted when the ASR is unusable if one of the listed positioning or handoff conditions is met.

choices.text: Continue conducting the PAR approach under the listed exception.
Suspend all PAR approaches until the ASR is restored because there are no exceptions.
Continue only if the aircraft has already passed decision height.
Continue only if the NAVAID fix is displayed exactly as required by paragraph 5-3-2 with no note-based exception.

items.explanation: Per 5-12-11, a direct radar handoff from an adjacent radar facility is one of the listed conditions that allows PAR approaches when ASR is unusable.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-2, when separate on-off controls are provided, which condition is one listed basis for operating REIL?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-2a, REIL are operated when the associated runway lights are lighted.

choices.text: When the associated runway lights are lighted.
Only when the approach controller requests them.
Only when the departure is already clear of the traffic pattern.
Only when visibility is less than 1 mile.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-2, when separate on-off controls are provided, REIL are turned off after a departing aircraft has left the traffic pattern area.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-4-2a2.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-7, which is one listed basis for operating sequenced flashing lights?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-7c, SFL may be operated as you deem necessary if not contrary to the pilot's request.

choices.text: As you deem necessary, if not contrary to the pilot's request.
Only when the ALS is extinguished.
Only after the aircraft passes the approach lights.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-7, sequenced flashing lights cannot be operated when the ALS is off.
items.explanation: That explanatory note appears in 3-4-7.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-8, what is required for a two-step MALS/ODALS system at a part-time tower if duplicate controls are not provided in the associated FSS during hours of darkness when the tower is not staffed?
items.explanation: The note under Table 3-4-6 requires the MALSR/ODALS to be set to low intensity in that situation.

choices.text: Set the MALSR/ODALS to low intensity.
Set the MALSR/ODALS to high intensity.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-8, facility directives may specify other MALSR/ODALS settings to meet local atmospheric, topographic, and twilight conditions.
items.explanation: That exception appears in 3-4-8a.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-9, when is the SSALR system operated?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-9b, SSALR is operated when the conditions in subparagraph a are not a factor.

choices.text: When the conditions in subparagraph a are not a factor.
Only when RVR is 4,000 feet or less.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-9, when the low-visibility conditions in subparagraph a are present, the ALSF-2 system may be operated as you deem necessary if not contrary to the pilot's request.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-4-9a2.

items.question_text: Under Table 3-4-8, which HIRL/RCLS/TDZL step applies during the day when visibility is 1 to but not including 2 miles?
items.explanation: Table 3-4-8 shows step 4 for day visibility 1 to but not including 2 miles.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-11, a facility directive may specify other settings to meet local conditions.
items.explanation: That exception appears in 3-4-11a.

items.question_text: Under Table 3-4-9, which HIRL-associated-with-MALSR step applies during the day when visibility is 1 to but not including 2 miles?
items.explanation: Table 3-4-9 shows step 4 for day visibility 1 to but not including 2 miles.

items.question_text: Under the note in 3-4-12, when lowering the brightness setting, rotate the control below the intended step and then back to the appropriate step to ensure proper MALSR brightness.
items.explanation: That note appears in 3-4-12.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-13, who should be kept informed, in advance if possible, of HIRL changes that affect RVR?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires keeping the appropriate approach controller or PAR controller informed.

choices.text: The appropriate approach controller or PAR controller.
Only the local controller working ground movement.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-13, the appropriate center controller should be informed, in advance if possible, of HIRL changes that affect RVR.
items.explanation: The paragraph says the appropriate approach controller or PAR controller, not the center controller.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-1, when should the aircraft be informed that it is approaching glidepath?
items.explanation: The paragraph says approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent.

choices.text: Approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent.
At the moment final descent begins.
After touchdown if the pilot requests it.

items.question_text: The phraseology for 5-12-1 is APPROACHING GLIDEPATH.
items.explanation: That phraseology appears in 5-12-1.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-3, what instruction is issued when the aircraft reaches the point where final descent is to start?
items.explanation: The phraseology in 5-12-3 is BEGIN DESCENT.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-3, the descent instruction is issued when the aircraft reaches the point where final descent is to start.
items.explanation: That is the full requirement in 5-12-3.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-7, what advisory is given when the aircraft is passing over the approach lights?
items.explanation: The phraseology in 5-12-7b is OVER APPROACH LIGHTS.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-7, glidepath and course information prescribed in paragraph 5-12-4, subparagraphs a and b, continues until the aircraft passes over threshold.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-12-7a.

items.question_text: According to the note in 5-12-9, why should communications transfer instructions be delayed slightly until the aircraft is on the landing roll-out?
items.explanation: The note says the slight delay is to preclude diversion of the pilot's attention during transition and touchdown.

choices.text: To preclude diversion of the pilot's attention during transition and touchdown.
To wait for the pilot to request a new frequency.
To ensure the aircraft has cleared the airport movement area first.
To avoid contacting the terminal control function at all.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-9, communications transfer instructions should be delayed slightly until the aircraft is on the landing roll-out to avoid diverting the pilot's attention during transition and touchdown.
items.explanation: That note appears in 5-12-9.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-11, which condition allows a PAR approach to be conducted when the ASR is unusable?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists either a nonradar approach that positions the aircraft over a NAVAID or DME fix within precision radar coverage, or a direct radar handoff from an adjacent radar facility.

choices.text: An adjacent radar facility can provide a direct radar handoff to the PAR controller.
The pilot reports the runway in sight before the final segment begins.
The ASR has been unavailable for less than 30 minutes.
The controller elects to waive identification requirements entirely.

items.question_text: Under the note in 5-12-11, display of the NAVAID or DME fix in accordance with paragraph 5-3-2 is not required if the fix can be correlated on a PAR scope.
items.explanation: That note appears in 5-12-11.
